jdbc_connector_workflow.overview:
This workflow guides you through creating a custom JDBC connector for any database using the IBM Watson Data Platform Connect SDK. You can choose between two approaches:
1. Codeless Mode: Quick setup using a pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest) - Best for standard JDBC connector use cases - No code generation or build process required - Faster deployment
2. Developer Mode: Full customizable connector code generation - Complete control over connector implementation - Includes code generation, customization, testing, and building - Best for custom implementations
Both modes end with deployment to AWS ECS Fargate.

prerequisites.requirement: Access to the target database for testing
JDBC driver information for the target database

phase.name: deployment_approach_selection
phase.title: Choose Deployment Approach
phase.description: Select between Codeless mode (using pre-built Docker image) or Developer mode (generating customizable connector code). This choice determines the workflow path.

step.action: Ask user to choose deployment approach
step.question: Choose your deployment approach for the JDBC connector:

suggestions.suggest: Codeless - Use pre-built Docker image (quick setup, standard use cases)
Developer - Generate customizable connector code (custom implementations)

step.action: Process user selection and set workflow path

actions.action: Inform user about pre-built Docker image usage
Create directory structure for DSL file
Skip code generation phases (2-9)
Proceed directly to Phase 10 (Flight App) with pre-built image
Inform user about custom code generation
Proceed to Phase 1 (Information Gathering)
Follow complete workflow through all phases

codeless_mode_details.overview: Codeless mode uses the pre-built Docker image: ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest

benefits.benefit: Best for standard JDBC connector use cases

directory_structure.purpose: Store JDBC driver JAR for the connector

driver_handling.description: In Codeless mode, the JDBC driver JAR must be provided and saved to connectors-forge/codeless/lib/driver.jar

automatic_download.condition: Driver is available from Maven Central or public repositories
automatic_download.action: Bob will automatically download the driver JAR
automatic_download.supported_databases: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, H2, Derby, SQLite

manual_provision.condition: Driver is proprietary or requires license
manual_provision.action: Bob will prompt user for local driver JAR path
manual_provision.examples: Oracle, IBM Db2, Microsoft SQL Server (with license)

workflow_impact.skip: Phase 10: Flight App Generation (uses pre-built Docker image instead)
workflow_impact.execute: Phase 2: DSL Creation (includes driver JAR handling)

developer_mode_details.overview: Developer mode generates full customizable connector code from scratch

phase.name: information_gathering
phase.title: Gather Database Information
phase.description: Collect all necessary information about the target database before starting the connector creation process. For Codeless mode, also determine driver availability.

required_information.item: Connector label (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle Database)
JDBC driver Maven coordinates (group:artifact:version) - for automatic download
Default port number for the database
Whether database supports schemas, catalogs, or both

step.action: Ask user for connector name and label
step.question: What is the name of the database you want to create a connector for?
Do you have the JDBC driver JAR file available on your system, or would you like it to be automatically downloaded?

suggestions.suggest: I have the driver JAR at a local path
Automatically download the driver (for open-source databases)

step.note: This determines whether to copy from local path or download from Maven Central

step.action: Handle driver based on user selection

path.name: local_driver
path.condition: User has driver at local path

substeps.substep: Ask user for the full path to the driver JAR file
Ask user for driver Maven coordinates (group:artifact:version)
Download driver using curl from Maven Central

phase.name: dsl_creation
phase.title: Create DSL Configuration
phase.description: Create a database-specific DSL configuration file based on DSL-jdbc.json template. This file defines all connector properties, driver information, and customizations. Driver JAR handling is now done in Phase 1 for Codeless mode.

step.action: Create new DSL file in connectors-forge/codeless/jdbc/ directory
Fill in connector metadata using information from Phase 1

fields.field: connector_description: Brief description of the connector
driver_class_name: Fully qualified driver class from Phase 1

step.note: This is for reference only in Codeless mode; actual driver is in connectors-forge/codeless/lib/

phase.name: deployment_preparation
phase.title: Prepare for Deployment (Codeless Mode Only)
phase.description: After DSL creation, prepare for deployment by selecting deployment type and creating the connector-config.properties file with values from the DSL.

step.action: Ask user to select deployment type
step.question: Choose your deployment type for the JDBC connector:

suggestions.suggest: AWS - Deploy to AWS ECS Fargate
OpenShift - Deploy to Red Hat OpenShift

step.description: Copy connector-config.properties.template to connector-config.properties and populate it with values from the DSL file created in Phase 2.

substep.action: Read the DSL file to extract connector information
substep.note: Extract connector_name, connector_label, connector_description, and driver_class_name

mapping.source: Set based on deployment type: - AWS: /mnt/efs/driver.jar - OpenShift: /drivers/driver.jar

substep.success_message: ✅ connector-config.properties created successfully with values from DSL

step.action: Verify driver JAR is in place from Phase 1

validation.check: File size is greater than 0 bytes
Verify connector_label is user-friendly and descriptive
Ensure driver_class_name is correct for the database
Confirm jdbc_url template uses correct syntax
Validate driver_dependencies array is properly formatted
Confirm DSL file is saved in connectors-forge/codeless/jdbc/ directory

phase.name: label_confirmation
phase.title: Confirm Connector Label
phase.description: Ask the user to confirm the connector_label before proceeding, as this will be visible in the UI and should be user-friendly.

actions.action: Extract connector_label from the created DSL file
Use ask_followup_question to confirm with user
If user wants to change it, update the DSL file

phase.example_question: I've set the connector label to "{label}". This will be displayed in the Cloud Pak for Data UI. Is this label acceptable, or would you like to change it?

phase.name: code_generation
phase.title: Generate Connector Template Code
phase.description: Run the autoGenerateJavaJdbc Gradle task to generate the connector skeleton code from the DSL configuration.

step.action: Change to sdk-gen directory if not already there

step.note: The DSL file path is relative to sdk-gen directory

step.action: Wait for task completion and verify success

phase.name: dsl_relocation
phase.title: Move DSL to Connector Directory
phase.description: Move the DSL configuration file to the generated connector's resources directory for use in subsequent customization steps.

step.action: Create the target directory if it doesn't exist
Copy DSL file to connector directory
step.tool: execute_command with cp or use read_file + write_to_file

phase.name: connector_customization
phase.title: Customize Connector Code
phase.description: Run the customizeJdbcConnector task to apply DSL configuration to the generated connector code, updating driver class names, connection URLs, and properties.

what_it_does.update: Updates Connector.java with correct JDBC driver class name
Modifies getConnectionURL() method to build correct JDBC URL

phase.name: test_generation
phase.title: Generate Test Suite
phase.description: Generate comprehensive property validation tests for the connector using the generateJdbcTests task.

phase.name: code_formatting
phase.title: Apply Code Formatting
phase.description: Run spotlessApply to format all generated and customized code according to project standards.

phase.name: build
phase.title: Build Connector
phase.description: Build the connector without running tests to verify compilation and packaging.

phase.name: flight_app_generation
phase.title: Generate Flight App
phase.description:
Automatically generate a Flight app project that includes the newly created connector. This creates a deployable application with the connector integrated.
NOTE: This phase is ONLY executed in Developer mode. In Codeless mode, the pre-built Docker image (gloria2000/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-flight:latest) is used instead, so this phase is skipped.

step.note: This task automatically determines the connector name from the DSL file

success_indicators.indicator: Flight app directory created at subprojects/flight/
Connector dependency added to flight app build.gradle

step.action: Verify flight app can be built
step.note: This step is optional but recommended to verify the flight app is properly configured

what_it_does.action: Makes connector ready for Cloud Pak for Data integration

phase.name: deployment
phase.title: Deployment
phase.description: Deploy the connector to your chosen platform. This phase first asks the user to select their deployment type, then guides them through the appropriate deployment process based on their selection and mode (Codeless vs Developer).

step.action: Execute deployment based on selection from Phase 2.5
step.description: For Codeless mode, deployment type was already selected in Phase 2.5. Now execute the appropriate deployment script.

aws_deployment.condition: User selected AWS deployment in Phase 2.5

substep.action: Inform user about AWS deployment execution
substep.message:
Executing AWS deployment using the pre-built Docker image: ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest
The deployment script will: 1. Pull the pre-built Docker image 2. Push it to your AWS ECR 3. Deploy to ECS Fargate with your connector configuration

substep.note: The script uses: - connector-config.properties for connector configuration - aws-deployment.properties for AWS settings - driver.jar from connectors-forge/codeless/lib/

openshift_deployment.condition: User selected OpenShift deployment in Phase 2.5

substep.action: Inform user about OpenShift deployment execution
substep.message:
Executing OpenShift deployment using the pre-built Docker image: ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest
The deployment script will: 1. Pull the pre-built Docker image 2. Push it to your OpenShift registry 3. Deploy to OpenShift with your connector configuration

substep.note: The script uses: - connector-config.properties for connector configuration - openshift-deployment.properties for OpenShift settings - driver.jar from connectors-forge/codeless/lib/

step.action: Ask user to select deployment type
step.question: Choose your deployment type for the JDBC connector:

suggestions.suggest: Local Docker - Test on local machine

step.action: Process deployment type selection and execute appropriate deployment workflow

path.name: local_docker
path.title: Local Docker Deployment
path.description: Build and run connector locally using Docker for testing

substeps.substep: Run container with appropriate port mappings
Verify connector is accessible on localhost

path.name: serverless
path.title: Serverless Deployment
path.description: Deploy to cloud serverless platform (AWS ECS Fargate, etc.)

substeps.substep: Guide user to configure cloud provider settings

substep.action: Inform user about AWS ECS Fargate deployment
substep.message: The connector will be deployed to AWS ECS Fargate. You need to configure your AWS deployment settings in the aws-deployment.properties file.

substep.action: Guide user to configure AWS properties
substep.instructions: The deployment requires AWS configuration. You need to: 1. Copy aws-deployment.properties.template to aws-deployment.properties 2. Fill in your AWS details in aws-deployment.properties 3. Run the deployment script

substep.message:
Please fill in the following required fields in aws-deployment.properties: - AWS_ACCOUNT_ID: Your 12-digit AWS account ID - AWS_REGION: AWS region (e.g., us-east-1) - VPC_ID: Your VPC ID (e.g., vpc-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx) - SUBNET_ID: Your subnet ID (e.g., subnet-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx) - ECR_IMAGE_URI: ECR image URI (format: account-id.dkr.ecr.region.amazonaws.com/repository-name:tag) - ECS_CLUSTER: ECS cluster name (will be created if it doesn't exist)
Optional: You can also configure AWS credentials in the file, or use AWS CLI configured credentials.

substep.action: Wait for user confirmation that properties are configured
substep.question: Have you filled in all the required AWS configuration details in aws-deployment.properties?

suggestions.suggest: Yes, configuration is complete and ready to deploy
No, I need more time to configure

substep.action: Run the appropriate AWS deployment script based on mode

codeless_mode.note: The script will pull the pre-built Docker image (gloria2000/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-flight:latest), push it to ECR, and deploy to ECS Fargate with connector configuration from connector-config.env

developer_mode.note: The script will build the Docker image from your custom connector code, push it to ECR, and deploy to ECS Fargate

prerequisites.requirement: Cloud provider CLI installed and configured
Valid cloud provider credentials with appropriate permissions
Network configuration (VPC, subnet, etc.) set up

path.name: self_hosted
path.title: Self-Hosted Deployment
path.description: Deploy to your own server infrastructure

substeps.substep: Push image to your private registry
Provide deployment instructions for your server

path.name: openshift
path.title: OpenShift Deployment
path.description: Deploy to Red Hat OpenShift platform

substeps.substep: Verify deployment and provide access details

substep.message: The connector will be deployed to Red Hat OpenShift. You need to configure your OpenShift deployment settings in the openshift-deployment.properties file.

substep.action: Guide user to configure OpenShift properties
substep.instructions: The deployment requires OpenShift configuration. You need to: 1. Ensure you have access to an OpenShift cluster 2. Configure openshift-deployment.properties with your cluster details 3. Run the deployment script

substep.message:
Please fill in the following required fields in openshift-deployment.properties: - OPENSHIFT_SERVER: OpenShift API server URL - OPENSHIFT_TOKEN: Authentication token for OpenShift - OPENSHIFT_PROJECT: Project/namespace name - IMAGE_REGISTRY: Container image registry URL - APP_NAME: Application name for the connector
Optional: Additional configuration for routes, resource limits, etc.

substep.action: Wait for user confirmation that properties are configured
substep.question: Have you filled in all the required OpenShift configuration details in openshift-deployment.properties?

suggestions.suggest: Yes, configuration is complete and ready to deploy
No, I need more time to configure

substep.note: The script will build the container image, push it to the registry, and deploy to OpenShift

prerequisites.requirement: Docker or Podman installed and running
Valid OpenShift cluster access with appropriate permissions

completion_criteria.criterion: DSL configuration file created and validated

criterion_conditional.criterion: DSL moved to connector resources directory
Flight app generated with connector integrated
Directory structure created for DSL file and driver JAR
Driver JAR obtained (downloaded or copied from local)

developer_mode.step: Review generated connector code in subprojects/jdbc_{database}/
Review generated flight app in subprojects/flight/

common_steps.step: Configure connector in Cloud Pak for Data using the gRPC endpoint

error.action: Check Gradle output for specific error messages
Verify DSL file syntax is valid JSON
Ensure all required fields are filled in DSL
Check that working directory is correct (sdk-gen/)
Consult database documentation for JDBC driver details
Check Maven Central for driver coordinates

error.description: Driver JAR could not be downloaded from Maven Central (Codeless mode)

actions.action: Verify driver coordinates are correct (group:artifact:version)
Check internet connectivity
Verify Maven Central is accessible (https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/)
Try alternative download method (curl/wget directly)
Check if driver requires authentication or license acceptance
If driver is proprietary, request local path from user

error.fallback: Ask user to provide driver JAR from local system

error.description: Driver JAR could not be copied to target location (Codeless mode)

actions.action: Verify source file path exists and is accessible
Check file is a valid JAR file (not corrupted)
Verify target directory exists: sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_jdbc/src/main/resources/
Create target directory if missing: mkdir -p sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_jdbc/src/main/resources/
Check file permissions for read/write access
Verify sufficient disk space available
Try copying with explicit permissions: cp -f source destination

error.description: Driver JAR not found at expected location for Codeless mode deployment

actions.action: Verify driver.jar exists at: sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_jdbc/src/main/resources/driver.jar
Check if driver download/copy step completed successfully
Review previous step outputs for errors
Re-run driver provisioning step (Phase 2, Step 6)
Verify file was not accidentally deleted or moved
Check file permissions and ownership

error.description: Provided driver JAR is invalid, corrupted, or incompatible (Codeless mode)

actions.action: Verify file is a valid JAR file (check file extension is .jar)
Check file size is greater than 0 bytes
Try extracting JAR contents to verify integrity: jar -tf driver.jar
Verify JAR contains expected JDBC driver class
Check if JAR is compatible with Java version being used
Request user to provide a different driver JAR from official source
Download driver from official vendor website if available

error.action: Check for compilation errors in generated code
Verify JDBC driver dependency is correct

actions.action: Verify deployment platform CLI/tools are installed and configured
Check credentials and permissions for the selected platform
Ensure network configuration is correct (VPC, subnet, firewall rules, etc.)
Verify Docker is running (if applicable)
Review deployment script/command output for specific errors
Check image registry access and URI format
Verify resource quotas and limits on the platform
Consult platform-specific documentation for troubleshooting
